text: The patient is positioned laterally with the right side up. A left-sided 12mm operating port is being placed in a fixed position, located three finger-breadths superior or medial to the anterior superior iliac spine.

The surgeon places a fixed port in the midclavicular line just below the costal margin, typically a five millimeter port. The camera is positioned to triangulate between this port and the previously placed left-sided port.

The surgeon makes a superficial incision in the posterior peritoneum, just lateral to the reflection of the mesocolon, carefully avoiding transgression of the Gerota's fascia to facilitate easier retraction of the colon and subsequent dissection.

The surgeon makes an initial incision on the anterior surface of the lower pole of the kidney using a harmonic scalpel, targeting the point of reflection of the mesocolon. No further lateral incision is performed.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision along the edge of the liver, allowing the liver to fall forward due to gravity. The surgical field is visible without the need for a fourth port at this stage.

The surgeon divides the peritoneal layer, retracts the lower pole of the kidney laterally, and applies tension to separate the peritoneum from the fascia overlying Girota's fat. Minor adhesions at this site are divided using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon uses the dominant right hand to perform dissection while the left hand retracts the lower pole of the kidney laterally, creating space between the lower pole and the inferior vena cava (IVC). The incision over Girota's fascia exposes the duodenum, gonadal vein, and ureter.

The surgeon identifies the ureter lateral to the gonadal vein within the triangle of dissection. The ureter becomes visible, allowing the surgeon to safely place an instrument beneath it and lift it without risking detachment of the gonadal vein from the inferior vena cava.
The surgeon applies clips to the ureter and proceeds to divide it, completing the separation of the ureter from surrounding structures.
The surgeon incises Girota's fascia to the lateral wall, retracting Girota's fat along with the kidney, ensuring the fat remains in continuity with the kidney to serve as an oncological barrier.

The surgeon divides the lateral peritoneal attachments of the kidney, ensuring the kidney remains stable with retraction from the left hand. This maneuver frees the lateral margin of the kidney, facilitating access to the renal hilum.

The surgeon lifts the lower pole of the kidney superiorly and laterally, creating tension on the tissue within the triangle bordered by the lower margin of the renal vein and the lateral aspect of the inferior vena cava (IVC). The harmonic scalpel is used to gently stroke the edge of the right renal vein, freeing adherent tissue atraumatically. The renal artery is sought behind the right renal vein, with the scalpel's strokes exposing the artery.

The surgeon attempts to locate the renal artery, which is expected to be positioned behind the renal vein. However, the artery is not easily visible, prompting the incision of the Girota's fascia in front of the renal vein to expose its anterior surface. The incision is extended along the liver margin.

The surgeon identifies a small knuckle of tissue above the superior edge of the renal vein, revealing the renal artery. Beyond this, a second large branch of the renal vein is visible, with the renal artery protruding between these two large branches, both originating from the IVC.
The surgeon further divides the Girota's fascia above the renal vein, increasing the mobility of the renal hilum while carefully avoiding the superior portion of the inferior vena cava (IVC).

The surgeon explores behind the renal vein, cleaning the fascia overlying the veins to safely approach the renal artery. The renal artery is mobilized for division, with preference for dividing it behind the renal vein, especially in a right-sided donor nephrectomy where the aim is to take the renal artery behind the IVC.

The surgeon uses a right-angle instrument to carefully divide adhesions between the renal vessels, ensuring not to inadvertently injure the vein. With two renal veins present, one is divided while the other remains to drain the kidney. Locking clips are applied and slid into position to secure the vein.

The surgeon applies the first clip easily to the renal vein, but encounters difficulty with the second clip, which fails to lock. This issue may arise if tissue is caught in the back of the clip or if the clip is improperly loaded in the clipper plier. The surgeon ensures two clips are placed on the patient side of the vein and one on the specimen side.
The surgeon divides the vein to access the renal artery located behind the residual renal vein. Fat overlying the artery is gently stroked to free adhesions, using suction for atraumatic dissection.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect tissue on the lateral aspect of the renal artery, which is adherent to the residual renal vein. The medial aspect of the artery remains unexposed.

The surgeon exposes the renal artery, creating a space between the artery and the vein. A linear stapler is applied, placing three rows of staples without cutting. A locking clip is placed medially on the artery, followed by an additional clip on the renal side. The artery is then divided.

The surgeon tests for back filling of the renal vein by clamping it, observing no obvious filling. The renal artery is divided lateral to the staple line. A small vasoarterium is cut just beyond the artery, with retraction maintained to apply appropriate clips.

The surgeon applies three locking clips to the residual renal vein, which is then divided between the lateral-most clips. A small bleeding vasoarterium is controlled with a single clip. The procedure progresses with dissection of the upper pole.

It's important when seeing bleeding to remain calm and to maintain a good view. Retraction is important, as is the position of the camera. It's tempting on behalf of the cameraman should the camera become splashed with blood to immediately remove the camera for cleaning. I would always discourage that and wash the camera with the suction irrigation. And that's something that we will always choose to do rather than remove the camera. This maintains the continuity of the surgery.

The surgeon assesses a visible bleeding point and decides on the method of control. For small delicate vessels, a clip is applied to control bleeding, avoiding the use of harmonic scalpel or diathermy. For larger vessels, such as the side wall of a renal vein, metallic clips are applied in a line to control bleeding. Extensive bleeding may require a large clip or a linear stapler, which is used to assess vessels before division.

You'll note that this surgery has been performed without the need for a fourth port and a fourth port is rare, providing good retraction is applied with the left hand. The position of the camera port is very dependent on the body mass index of the patient and I would certainly ignore the umbilicus as a landmark.

The surgeon places the camera port using a Hassan cut-down technique at a third point of a triangle formed by the iliac fossa port and the midclavicular subcostal port. In an obese patient, this port may be positioned very laterally on the abdominal wall. After detaching the kidney from the lateral abdominal wall, the surgeon inspects the hilum and aspirates any residual back bleeding.
This includes feeding on the evening after surgery. mobilisation on the evening after surgery and active encouragement to dress and be mobile. We aim for discharge the day after their procedure.

The surgeon delivers the kidney through an extended iliac fossa incision, using an endoscopic bag placed within the abdomen to scoop the kidney into the bag. The surgical wounds are then closed in a standard manner, completing the procedure.
